section.title: Zacchaeus and the Parable of the Minas
section.sectionRationale: In Jericho, a despised chief tax collector climbs a tree to see Jesus and is stunned to hear the Lord invite Himself to his house; Zacchaeus repents with joy, and Jesus declares that salvation has come, for the Son of Man came to seek and to save the lost. Then, because the crowd expects the kingdom to appear at once, Jesus tells of a nobleman who goes to receive a kingdom and returns to reckon with servants entrusted with money and with citizens who rejected him. Grace saves the lost, and the King who is going away will surely return to judge.
section.thesis: The Son of Man seeks and saves the lost, transforming even a chief tax collector by grace, and He is the King who goes to receive His kingdom and will return to reward faithfulness, judge fruitless fear, and destroy those who refused His reign.
section.frameNote: Two movements: the salvation of Zacchaeus; and the parable of the ten minas, on faithfulness while the King is away and the reckoning at His return.

units.label: The salvation of Zacchaeus
units.note: the Son of Man came to seek and to save the lost

verses.text: Then Jesus entered Jericho and was passing through.
And there was a man named Zacchaeus, a chief tax collector, who was very wealthy.
He was trying to see who Jesus was, but could not see over the crowd because he was small in stature.
So he ran on ahead and climbed a sycamore tree to see Him, since Jesus was about to pass that way.
When Jesus came to that place, He looked up and said, “Zacchaeus, hurry down, for I must stay at your house today.”
So Zacchaeus hurried down and welcomed Him joyfully.
And all who saw this began to grumble, saying, “He has gone to be the guest of a sinful man!”
But Zacchaeus stood up and said to the Lord, “Look, Lord, half of my possessions I give to the poor, and if I have cheated anyone, I will repay it fourfold.”
Jesus said to him, “Today {{soteria|salvation}} has come to this house, because this man too is a son of Abraham.
For the Son of Man came to {{zeteo|seek}} and to save the {{apollymi|lost}}.”

units.label: The parable of the ten minas
units.note: well done, good servant; and, slay my enemies

verses.text: While the people were listening to this, Jesus proceeded to tell them a parable, because He was near Jerusalem and they thought the kingdom of God would appear imminently.
So He said, “A man of noble birth went to a distant country to lay claim to his kingship and then return.
Beforehand, he called ten of his servants and gave them ten minas. ‘Conduct business with this until I return,’ he said.
But his subjects hated him and sent a delegation after him to say, ‘We do not want this man to rule over us.’
When he returned from procuring his kingship, he summoned the servants to whom he had given the money, to find out what each one had earned.
The first servant came forward and said, ‘Master, your mina has produced ten more minas.’
His master replied, ‘Well done, good servant! Because you have been {{pistos|faithful}} in a very small matter, you shall have authority over ten cities.’
The second servant came and said, ‘Master, your mina has made five minas.’
And to this one he said, ‘You shall have authority over five cities.’
Then another servant came and said, ‘Master, here is your mina, which I have laid away in a piece of cloth.
For I was afraid of you, because you are a harsh man. You withdraw what you did not deposit and reap what you did not sow.’
His master replied, ‘You wicked servant, I will judge you by your own words. So you knew that I am a harsh man, withdrawing what I did not deposit and reaping what I did not sow?
Why then did you not deposit my money in the bank, and upon my return I could have collected it with interest?’
Then he told those standing by, ‘Take the mina from him and give it to the one who has ten minas.’
‘Master,’ they said, ‘he already has ten!’
He replied, ‘I tell you that everyone who has will be given more; but the one who does not have, even what he has will be taken away from him.
And these enemies of mine who were unwilling for me to rule over them, bring them here and slay them in front of me.’”

soteria.gloss: Salvation, rescue, deliverance. Jesus announces that today salvation has come to Zacchaeus's house, not as a reward for the man's repayment but as the gracious arrival of God's rescue in the person of Jesus. The changed life that follows is the evidence, not the cause, of a salvation that has come.

zeteo.gloss: To seek, to look for. The mission statement of the Gospel, the Son of Man came to seek and to save the lost, casts Jesus as the seeking Savior. Zacchaeus climbs a tree to see Jesus, but it is Jesus who has been seeking him; the initiative and the search belong to God.

apollymi.gloss: To be lost, to perish. The lost are those perishing away from God, and Zacchaeus, rich yet despised and cut off, is exactly such a one. Jesus came for people like him, not for those who imagine they are found, and He restores this lost son of Abraham to the family of faith.

pistos.gloss: Faithful, trustworthy. The servants who put the master's mina to work are praised as faithful in a very small matter and given authority over cities. Faithfulness with what the absent King entrusts, however small it seems, is what He will reward at His return; fearful inaction is judged as wickedness.

basic.context: As Jesus passes through Jericho, Zacchaeus, a wealthy chief tax collector despised as a traitor and cheat, climbs a sycamore tree just to catch sight of Him. Jesus stops, looks up, and invites Himself to stay at the man's house. The crowd grumbles that Jesus is the guest of a sinner, but Zacchaeus, transformed, pledges half his goods to the poor and fourfold repayment to anyone he has cheated. Jesus declares that salvation has come to this house, for the Son of Man came to seek and to save the lost. Then, because the crowd expects the kingdom to arrive instantly as they near Jerusalem, Jesus tells of a nobleman who leaves to receive a kingdom, entrusts money to his servants, and returns to reward the faithful, judge the servant who hid his mina in fear, and destroy the citizens who refused his rule.
basic.hermeneutics: The two scenes belong together. Zacchaeus shows what salvation looks like: grace seeks the lost, and repentance and generosity follow. The parable corrects the crowd's timing: the kingdom will not appear all at once; the King must go away, and there will be a long interval of faithful stewardship before He returns to reckon and to reign.
basic.originalLanguages: Jesus announces soteria, salvation, has come to Zacchaeus's house, God's rescue arriving in person. He states His mission: the Son of Man came to zeteo, to seek, and to save the apollymi, the lost, those perishing away from God. And in the parable the servants judged good are those who were pistos, faithful, with the little entrusted to them while the King was away.
basic.typology: Zacchaeus, a son of Abraham restored, pictures the true children of Abraham marked by faith, not merely descent. The nobleman going to a far country to receive a kingdom and returning echoes both the historical Herods and, more deeply, Christ's ascension and second coming. The rejected king points to the Messiah his own people would not have reign over them.
basic.christ: Jesus is the Son of Man who seeks and saves the lost, the friend of sinners whose mere presence brings salvation into a house. And He is the noble King who goes to a distant country to receive His kingship, entrusts His servants with responsibility, and will return to reward, to judge, and to reign, whether His subjects welcome Him or refuse Him.
basic.god: God takes the initiative to save, seeking out the very people the religious despise. He is generous, entrusting real responsibility to His servants, and just, rewarding faithfulness and judging fruitless fear and outright rebellion. His salvation is free, and His reign is certain.
basic.crossReferences: Ezekiel 34 promises that God will seek the lost sheep. Luke 15 tells three parables of the lost being found. Matthew 25 gives the parallel parable of the talents. Acts 1 recounts the King going away, to return as He left.
basic.oneStory: Just before Jerusalem, Jesus embodies His whole mission by saving a lost tax collector, then warns that the kingdom will not dawn instantly; the King must go and return. The story runs from grace that seeks the lost to the certain reckoning of the King, so His people live faithfully in the interval, longing for His reign.

context.literary: Zacchaeus is the climax of Luke's theme of Jesus and tax collectors and sinners, and a deliberate contrast with the rich ruler of chapter 18 who would not part with his wealth. The saying in verse 10 sums up the Gospel. The parable of the minas transitions the narrative toward Jerusalem and the passion, tempering the crowd's premature messianic excitement.
context.historical: Jericho was a prosperous customs center, so a chief tax collector there would be very rich and deeply resented as a collaborator with Rome. Fourfold restitution exceeded what the law required for most thefts, a sign of real repentance. The parable draws on recent memory: Archelaus, son of Herod, had traveled to Rome to receive his kingship while his subjects sent a delegation opposing him.
context.canonical: The seeking God who saves the lost runs from Ezekiel 34 through Luke 15 to this verse. The entrusting of resources and the reckoning at the master's return is a recurring parabolic pattern that shapes Christian expectation of the second coming. Being a true son of Abraham by faith is developed by Paul in Romans and Galatians.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To display the heart of Jesus' mission in the salvation of a lost sinner by grace, and to correct the expectation of an instant kingdom by teaching that the King must depart to receive His reign and will return to reward faithful servants, judge the fearful and fruitless, and destroy those who reject His rule.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The Zacchaeus narrative describes a real conversion but models the repentance and generosity that grace produces. The parable is illustrative, not an endorsement of literal violence against enemies, yet it prescribes faithful stewardship and warns truthfully of judgment for rejecting the King.

debates.question: Is Zacchaeus's promise the cause or the fruit of his salvation?

views.label: The fruit and evidence of grace already received
views.case: Jesus says salvation has come because Zacchaeus is a son of Abraham, restored by the Lord's gracious initiative, not because his giving earns it. The generosity flows from a heart already changed by being sought and found; grace comes first, and repentance answers it.

views.label: A vindication of genuine repentance
views.case: Some read Zacchaeus's declaration as the decisive turning that Jesus then approves, stressing that saving faith is inseparable from real restitution and changed conduct. The salvation is confirmed precisely in the fruit that proves it is no empty profession.

debates.bearing: Either way the order of grace holds: Jesus sought and welcomed Zacchaeus first, and true salvation shows itself in the open-handed, restoring life that follows.

debates.question: How should the parable's harsh ending, 'slay them in front of me', be understood?

views.label: A sober picture of judgment on those who reject the King
views.case: The citizens who said 'we do not want this man to rule over us' stand for those who refuse Christ's reign, and the ending portrays, in the stark terms of an ancient king's return, the reality of final judgment. It is a parabolic image, not a license for human vengeance.

views.label: A warning aimed first at Jerusalem
views.case: Told as Jesus nears the city that will reject Him, the parable foreshadows the coming judgment on Jerusalem and its leaders who would not have Him as King, dramatized soon after in His tears over the city and the temple's fate.

debates.bearing: Both readings press the same truth: to refuse the reign of the returning King is no small thing, and the interval of His absence is the time to receive Him and serve Him faithfully.

study.originalLanguages: Jesus announces that soteria, salvation, God's own rescue, has come to Zacchaeus's house today, arriving in the person of the Savior who has entered under his roof. He then states the mission that gathers up the whole Gospel: the Son of Man came to zeteo, to seek, and to save the apollymi, the lost, those perishing far from God, of whom a hated, wealthy tax collector is a prime example. The search and the initiative are Christ's; Zacchaeus only climbed a tree, but Jesus had been seeking him. In the parable that follows, the servants commended at the King's return are those who proved pistos, faithful, with the small sum entrusted to them, and were given authority over cities, while the fearful servant who hid his mina is condemned. Grace seeks and saves; faithfulness with what the absent King entrusts is what He will reward.

typology.type: God seeking His lost sheep, Ezekiel 34:16
typology.fulfillment: The Son of Man come to seek and to save the lost, embodied in the finding of Zacchaeus, Luke 19:10.

typology.type: A nobleman going to receive a kingdom and returning
typology.fulfillment: Christ ascending to receive His kingship and returning to reign and to judge, Luke 19:12-27.

typology.type: The true son of Abraham marked by faith
typology.fulfillment: Zacchaeus, a tax collector, declared a son of Abraham by the grace that changed him, Luke 19:9.

study.christ: Jesus is the seeking Savior whose settled mission is to find and rescue the lost, and He proves it by stopping under a sycamore tree for a man everyone else had written off. His presence alone brings salvation into a home, and He is unashamed to be called the guest of a sinner. In the parable He unveils Himself as the noble King who must go to a distant country, His ascension, to receive His kingdom, entrusting His servants with responsibility in the interval and promising to return. At that return He will reward the faithful with authority, expose the fearful and fruitless, and judge those who refused His reign. The Christ who saves the lost is the same Christ who will come again as King.
study.god: The God of this passage moves first to save, seeking out the despised and the lost rather than waiting for the worthy. He is generous, pouring salvation into a sinner's house and entrusting His servants with real stakes in His kingdom. He is just, delighting to reward faithfulness with yet greater responsibility, but refusing to overlook the fearful burying of what He gave or the rebellion that will not have Him reign. His grace and His judgment are not rivals; the same King who welcomes Zacchaeus will settle every account when He returns.

crossReferences.note: I will seek the lost and bring back the strayed, the promise fulfilled in the Son of Man seeking Zacchaeus.
The parables of the lost sheep and coin, the same seeking, saving joy embodied in Zacchaeus.
The parallel parable of the talents, entrusted resources and a reckoning at the master's return.
The King ascends and will return in the same way, the pattern behind the nobleman who leaves to receive a kingdom.

study.oneStory: On the threshold of Jerusalem Jesus lives out His mission by seeking and saving a lost tax collector, showing that grace reaches the very people religion despises. Then He tempers the crowd's dream of an instant kingdom: the King must go to receive His reign and will return to reckon with His servants and His enemies. The story runs from the sycamore tree of grace to the throne of the returning King, calling His people to faithful stewardship until He comes.

quiz.q: What does Jesus declare about Zacchaeus?

options.text: That salvation has come to his house, for the Son of Man came to seek and to save the lost.
options.note: Luke 19:9-10 announces salvation and states Jesus' seeking, saving mission.

options.text: That Zacchaeus earned his place by giving to the poor.
options.note: The giving is the fruit of salvation already come, not the price that earns it.

options.text: That Zacchaeus is a true son of Abraham, restored by grace.
options.note: True and stated, but it omits the climactic mission statement that the Son of Man seeks and saves the lost.

options.text: That Jesus should not have entered a sinner's house.
options.note: That is the crowd's grumble, which Jesus overturns by declaring salvation.

quiz.q: How does Zacchaeus respond to Jesus?

options.text: He welcomes Jesus joyfully and pledges half his goods to the poor and fourfold repayment to any he cheated.
options.note: Luke 19:6-8 shows joyful welcome and concrete, generous repentance.

options.text: He promises to give generously to the poor.
options.note: True, but it omits the fourfold restitution and the joyful welcome that mark his repentance.

options.text: He feels guilty but keeps his wealth.
options.note: The opposite; he gladly parts with his wealth in genuine repentance.

options.text: He hides from Jesus in shame.
options.note: He climbed a tree to see Jesus and welcomed Him joyfully.

quiz.q: Why does Jesus tell the parable of the ten minas?

options.text: Because the crowd thought the kingdom would appear immediately, so He teaches that the King must go and return.
options.note: Luke 19:11 gives the reason: to correct expectations of an instant kingdom.

options.text: To teach that the kingdom's arrival was delayed.
options.note: True in effect, but it misses that He specifically addresses their expectation of an immediate kingdom.

options.text: To warn against investing money carelessly.
options.note: The minas illustrate stewardship of what the King entrusts, not financial advice.

options.text: To announce that the kingdom had already fully come.
options.note: The parable corrects that very idea; the King departs before returning to reign.

quiz.q: What distinguishes the faithful servants from the third servant?

options.text: The faithful put the mina to work and are rewarded, while the third hid it in fear and is condemned.
options.note: Luke 19:16-24 rewards faithful labor and judges fearful inaction.

options.text: The faithful earned more money than the third servant.
options.note: True on the surface, but the deeper issue is faithful stewardship versus fearful, fruitless hiding.

options.text: The third servant lost the mina entirely.
options.note: He kept it safe in a cloth; his fault was fearful inaction, not losing it.

options.text: The master rewarded all the servants equally.
options.note: Rewards matched faithfulness, and the fearful servant lost even his mina.

quiz.q: What does the fate of the rebellious citizens picture?

options.text: The judgment awaiting those who refuse the reign of the returning King.
options.note: Luke 19:14,27 portrays, in an ancient king's terms, the judgment on those who reject his rule.

options.text: That rejecting the King brings serious consequences.
options.note: True but soft; the parable pictures decisive judgment, not merely consequences.

options.text: A call for Jesus' followers to punish His enemies.
options.note: It is a parabolic image of divine judgment, not a mandate for human vengeance.

options.text: That the King is powerless against those who oppose Him.
options.note: The opposite: the King returns with full authority to judge.

questions: Zacchaeus only wanted to see Jesus, but Jesus was seeking him. Where in your story can you see that God was searching for you before you ever reached for Him?
Salvation showed itself in Zacchaeus's open hands and honest restitution. If grace has truly reached you, where should it be reshaping how you handle money and past wrongs?
The crowd wanted the kingdom to appear instantly. Where are you impatient for God to act now, and how does the promise of the returning King steady you in the meantime?
The faithful servants put a little to work; the fearful one buried it. What has the King entrusted to you, and are you investing it or hiding it out of fear?
The citizens said, we do not want this man to rule over us. Is there any area of your life where you are quietly refusing the reign of the King?

translationNotes.note: A mina was a sum of money, likely a silver coin worth about a hundred days' wages; each servant received one. The equal starting amount, unlike the varied talents of Matthew 25, sharpens the focus on faithfulness with the same trust.

The picture of a nobleman traveling to a distant country to receive kingship recalls Archelaus, who went to Rome for his crown while a delegation of his subjects opposed him. Jesus uses the familiar scene to speak of His own departure and return.

'A son of Abraham' declares Zacchaeus a true heir of the promise, not by his descent, which he already had, but by the faith and repentance that grace produced, a theme the New Testament develops for all who believe.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Luke 19:1-27. Headings, line breaks, footnote markers, and speaker labels were skipped when assembling verse text; no em or en dashes occur in this passage. Sermon status set to none; no sermon from the trusted cluster was verified this session.
